message.content:
I need to compare how much ranking information is extracted per provider call for these two methods.

**A (whole_list_sort):** The model receives all items and all attributes/weights at once, then returns a single complete ranked list. In one call, you get a full ordering of N items, which contains N-1 bits of ordering information (or log2(N!) bits of total ranking information). This is highly efficient per call.

**B (bandit_policy_cardinal):** This uses pairwise comparisons with a bandit policy. Each call compares two items, yielding one pairwise cardinal judgment. Even with an intelligent policy that targets high-uncertainty pairs, each call still only resolves information about one pair. The bandit policy improves *which* comparisons to make (efficiency of the sequence), but each individual call still extracts only pairwise information.

**Comparison:**
- A extracts a full ranking of N items in a single call: O(N log N) bits of information
- B extracts one pairwise comparison per call: O(1) bits of information per call

For even modest N (say N=10), A extracts roughly log2(10!) ≈ 21.8 bits per call, while B extracts roughly log2(possible cardinal outcomes) per call — maybe a few bits at most.

The ratio scales with N. For typical ranking tasks (N=10-20 items), A provides roughly 10-20x more information per call. The bandit policy in B is smart about *which* pairs to compare but doesn't change the per-call information yield. I'll estimate a ratio around 10-15x for typical use cases.

```json
{"higher_id":"A","ratio":12.0,"confidence":0.72}
```
